frontmatter.title: Start learning SQL in Google Colab

children.value: In this example, we will start using SQL in Google Colab Notebook.
But first, let’s just review some basics of Google Colab.

Let’s start with just running a bit of “code.” The cell I am currently is a “Text” cell (using +Text). For code, we want to use a “code” cell (+Code). The below statement will just add two numbers together, nothing too fancy.
Click on the “+Code” button in the top-left corner.
Figure out the answer for the following math problem.

children.html: (102939129381/231)+12300(102939129381 / 231) + 12300(102939129381/231)+12300

children.value: One of the cool tricks you can do in Jupyter Notebooks is something so amazing, you might even think its magic! Well, it technically is.
Jupyter has a number of built in utilities, which they call
. Don’t worry about trying to learn them all, and we only need one specific type of magic. SQL magic. Actually, we technically need %load_ext magic, but this will load SQL for us.

import piplite
await piplite.install('ipython-sql')
import ipython-sql
await piplite.install('sqlalchemy')
import sqlalchemy

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
The sql extension is already loaded. To reload it, use:
  %reload_ext sql

children.value: This is a built-in Python SQL toolkit provide by (another magic reference)
. Again, you don’t need to worry too much about SQL Alchemy, only that we can now use SQL code in our notebook.

The next step is to create a database, so in this case, we use the following command. Note: we will be expanding on this throughout the semester, but for now... it creates a single, SQLite database for this notebook. Also, if the command runs

This is your database for adding tables and inserting data, BUT this is temporary. So, do not assume that this will save between sessions. If you want to save this database for later, go to the left (where the folder is), select the folder, and then download the database (right-click on the database).
Here is a list of common interactions with database.
Now it is time to create our first table. This is where the SQL commands become available to us. The first command is...
Create will create a table. In this case, we will call the table
, and we will need to tell the table the names of the attributes and what
of attribute they are as well. For now, we will be using only 3 types:
For us, we need 6 attributes, all of which will be text.
Let’s create 1 table for student information.
Now let’s add some data to this table. In this case we use...

Insert does as it sounds. Insert data into the database.
The syntax for this is:
For now, I will just make up a few tuples.
Also, let’s note something. When to use:

jupyter_data.text:
* sqlite:///studentDatabase.db
1 rows affected.
1 rows affected.
1 rows affected.

children.value: OK, we have a Database. A table in this database. 3 tuples in our table. The last thing we need is a way to access, view, and act on this data. That’s where we use...

text/html.content:
PSUID firstName lastName streetAddress city state gpa
9139128181 David Practice 123 Apple Street State College PA 3.78
9771271281 Susan Practice 452 Banana Avenue State College PA 3.12
9383818823 Lee Example 836 Carrot Circle Cleveland OH 2.68

text/plain.content:
[('9139128181', 'David', 'Practice', '123 Apple Street', 'State College', 'PA', 3.78),
 ('9771271281', 'Susan', 'Practice', '452 Banana Avenue', 'State College', 'PA', 3.12),
 ('9383818823', 'Lee', 'Example', '836 Carrot Circle', 'Cleveland', 'OH', 2.68)]

children.value: SELECT allows us to select all or part of the data. For now, all we want to do is get back from the table all the data in the table. For this we will use:
Other things we can do with SELECT.
Let’s say we want to know which database we are connected to and what tables we have created. We would use the following SQL.
Great! We created a table and put some data into the table, but how do we... destroy it?
DROP is used to “delete” the table and all tuples.
A table that is dropped is completely deleted, including ALL DATA. Be careful with this.

jupyter_data.name: stdout
jupyter_data.text:
* sqlite:///studentDatabase.db
(sqlite3.OperationalError) near "#DROP": syntax error
[SQL: #DROP TABLE Student;]
(Background on this error at: https://sqlalche.me/e/20/e3q8)

children.value: So, now we check all the tables we have...
Here are the additional commands will be learning about in the class (we will not cover all of these, but most)
What is the first and last name of students from PA?

%%sql
SELECT firstName,lastName,state FROM student WHERE state == "PA"  ;
What is the street address of students from Cleveland?

%%sql
sElEcT streetaddress FROM student WHERE city == "Cleveland"  ;
What are all the attributes of students from PA and with a GPA greater than 3.5?

%%sql
SELECT * FROM student WHERE state == "PA" AND gpa > 3.5;
Create at least 3 tuples for this table.
